coredata.dc:title: High fluorescent sulfur regulating graphene quantum dots with tunable photoluminescence properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Yi
Li, Ming
Sun, Lang
Xu, Yongjie
Li, Ming
Hu, Guanghui
Tang, Tao
Wen, Jianfeng
Li, Xinyu
Zhang, Jiye
Wang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) were synthesized by two facile hydrothermal technologies. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the GQDs and S-GQDs samples were mainly investigated. Through regulating the content of S powders in S-GQDs synthesizing process, the optimal S-GQDs have a high S/C atomic ratio of 19.53%. The S doping introduce more functional groups on the C sp 2 skeleton of S-3 sample and result in the appearance of the strong absorption band in the UV region. In comparison with other reported S-GQDs, the S-GQDs exhibit overwhelming high fluorescence quantum yield (57%) and excitation-independent emission, resulting from the outcome of the doped sulfur atoms. Moreover, the PL intensity of GQDs can be increased by doping it with S and the increasing efficiency depends on the thiophene sulfur content.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur regulating
High fluorescent
Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence

China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the reaction mechanism of S-GQDs.

AFM images of GQDs (a) and S-3 (b) (inset: height distributions and height profile along the white line). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GQDs (c) and S-3 (d). Insets are the corresponding lateral size distribution. Typical high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of GQDs (e) and S-3 (f). Single HRTEM image of GQDs (g) and S-3 (h).
(a) Raman spectra of GQDs and S-3, (b) XPS full spectra of GQDs and S-GQDs samples, (c) fine-scanned S 2p spectra of S-GQDs samples, (d) high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum of S-3.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the (a) GQDs and (b) S-3 sample dispersed in water, and the insets are the optical photographs of the corresponding samples under the excitation with the wavelength of 360 nm. Time resolved PL spectra of S-3 (c).
(a) PL spectra of S-3 with different excitation wavelength from 310 to 490 nm. (b) PL spectra of GQDs and S-GQDs samples under the irradiation with 390 nm wavelength.

(a) The distributions of the two S types for the S-GQDs samples obtained at different S powders weights. (b) The dependence of the enhanced efficiency on S powders weights. (c) The dependence of the enhanced efficiency on thiophene sulfur content.

(a) The proposed energy level diagram of the S-GQDs. (b) The proposed PL mission mechanisms of the emission in GQDs and S-3 from disorder-induced states: (i) the PL emission of GQDs from cluster-like states. (ii) The PL emission of S-3 from cluster-like states.
The atomic percent (at.%) of GQDs and S-GQDs samples resulted from XPS measurement (hydrogen was not in consideration).
The contents of S, Thiophene sulfur, and Oxide-sulfur in the S-GQDs samples.
Quantum yield of a series of S-GQDs samples using quinine sulfate as a reference.

head.ce:title: High fluorescent sulfur regulating graphene quantum dots with tunable photoluminescence properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Through regulating the content of S powders in S-GQDs synthesizing process, the optimal S-GQDs have a high S/C atomic ratio of 19.53%. The S doping introduce more functional groups on the C sp 2 skeleton of S-3 sample and result in the appearance of the strong absorption band in the UV region. In comparison with other reported S-GQDs, the S-GQDs exhibit overwhelming high fluorescence quantum yield (57%) and excitation-independent emission, resulting from the outcome of the doped sulfur atoms. Moreover, the PL intensity of GQDs can be increased by doping it with S and the increasing efficiency depends on the thiophene sulfur content.

Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) were synthesized by two facile hydrothermal technologies. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the GQDs and S-GQDs samples were mainly investigated. Through regulating the content of S powders in S-GQDs synthesizing process, the optimal S-GQDs have a high S/C atomic ratio of 19.53%. The S doping introduce more functional groups on the C sp 2 skeleton of S-3 sample and result in the appearance of the strong absorption band in the UV region. In comparison with other reported S-GQDs, the S-GQDs exhibit overwhelming high fluorescence quantum yield (57%) and excitation-independent emission, resulting from the outcome of the doped sulfur atoms. Moreover, the PL intensity of GQDs can be increased by doping it with S and the increasing efficiency depends on the thiophene sulfur content.

ce:section.ce:para: As caused by a myriad of appealing physicochemical properties, including good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, high quantum luminescent efficiency, and large-scale availability, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) comprise an emerging carbon nanomaterial that attracted tremendous research favor [1]. GQDs were exploited in multiple applications, including ion detection [2,3], bio-sensing [4,5], bio-imaging [6,7], and fuel cells [8]. Compared with other conventional semiconductors, including CdX (X = S, Se, Te) and PbS, GQDs exhibit no apparent toxicity in vitro and in vivo; this property shows potential applications in the area of biomedicine [9]. As a result of low quantum yield (QYs) and non-specificity of GQDs [10,11], restriction of their sensitivity and selectivity becomes a major disadvantage of GQD-based fluorescent materials.

To effectively modulate band gap and electronic density, to enhance chemical activity, and to expand practical applications of QYs of GQDs, modified GQDs were doped with heteroatoms, such as nitrogen [12], boron [13], chlorine [14], and sulfur [15], to effectively induce new phenomena and properties, as proven through theoretical calculations and detailed experiments [16–18]. Doping of heteroatoms in GQD framework benefits heteroatom-doped GQD materials; nevertheless, reports rarely discuss sulfur-doped GQDs (S-GQDs), and their unique optical properties are virtually unknown [19]. Compared with other heteroatoms, sulfur (S) atom is markedly larger than carbon (C) atom. The difference in electronegativity between S (2.58) and C (2.55) [20] is so small that there can’t be obvious charger transfer in CS composites. Thus, difficulty arises from chemical doping of S atom into the framework of GQDs. Recently, Yang et al. [21] synthesized sulfur-doped graphene by directly annealing graphene oxide and benzyl disulfide in argon; doped graphene exhibited better catalytic activity than commercial catalysts. Therefore, an approach for preparing S-GQDs with high crystal quality should be developed. Li et al. [22] prepared S-GQDs through a hydrothermal approach with fructose and sulfuric acid as source materials and proved that incorporation of ∼1 at.% of S atom into QDs could effectively modify electronic structure of S-GQDs by introducing S-related energy levels between π and π* of C. Based on such observations, we deduced introduction of additional energy levels by sulfur doping, thus resulting in additional absorption peaks and tunable emission peaks. Given that most researches emphasized on pretreatment, renewed interest highlights on posttreatment to regulate luminescent properties through modifying S contents. Thus, further study on optical tunability of S-GQDs with different S content piques scientific advantage and potentially bear technical importance.

Herein, we report a facile post treatment hydrothermal technique to prepare the low-cost, high-yield S-GQDs. The PL properties of the GQDs and S-GQDs samples were discussed. The S-GQDs exhibit extremely high fluorescence quantum yield (57%) and excitation-independent PL behavior. Most interestingly, the PL intensity of GQDs can be increased by doping it with S and the increasing efficiency depends on the thiophene sulfur content.

GQDs and S-GQDs were prepared in large scales through a bottom-up method, as reported elsewhere [23,24]. Following a typical hydrothermal procedure for preparing S-GQDs, 300 mL obtained GQD solution and S powders with an average size of 5 μm (S weights: 0.12, 0.25, 0.375, 0.5, and 0.63g for S-1, S-2, S-3, S-4, and S-5 samples, respectively) were mixed to form a homogeneous solution, which was subsequently transferred into a Teflon autoclave. After heating to 180 °C in the oven for 12 h, the mixture containing water-soluble S-GQDs was filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon products. The obtained supernatant was collected and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) to expel excessive sulfate radicals and carbonate ions. Purified S-GQDs were dried at 80 °C for structural characterization and property measurement.

Morphologies of samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on ESCALAB 250Xi (TMAG, USA) with Al Kα radiation. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λex = 633 nm. Absorbance spectroscopy was characterized with a UV/Vis/NIR Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Lambda 750). PL spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent). All measurements were performed at room temperature.

The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) were measured with a monochromatic He I light source (21.2 eV) and a VG Scienta R4000 analyzer. A sample bias of −5 V was applied to observe the secondary electron cutoff (SEC). The work function (ϕ) can be determined by the difference between the photon energy and the binding energy of the secondary cutoff edge. The S-GQDs thin films were prepared from spin-coating on ITO substrates for UPS.

Fig. 1 illustrates a scheme for the growth of S-GQDs. Single-crystalline GQDs were formed by polymerization of pyrene. Subsequently, the obtained GQDs with plentiful OH, CO, and COOH groups on the edges formed S-GQDs by adding sulfur powder as S atom sources under hydrothermal conditions. With existence of the above oxygen-containing groups, doped sulfur was observed at the surface (or edges) of GQDs as two chemically active configurations: thiophene sulfur and oxide-sulfur, which can be corroborated from the analysis of XPS spectra in Fig. 3c and d.

Unless otherwise stated, S-GQDs (labeled as: S-3 sample) with the strongest optical performance were selected for various characterization. Fig. 2 a and b using AFM images displays the dispersivity of GQDs and S-3 on the mica sheet, in which yellow spots are regarded as GQDs and S-3 by their sizes. All of these panels in Fig. 2a and b reveal that GQDs and S-3 had merely several layers of graphene. The layer of S-3 is thicker than that of GQDs due to the agglomeration of GQDs in hydrothermal process for synthesizing the S-3. TEM showed well-dispersion of GQDs, S-3 (Fig. 2c and d) and their relatively narrow size distribution, which ranged from 3–10 nm to 1–7 nm, and the average diameter of 6 and 3 nm (insets of Fig. 2c and d), respectively. The average diameters of S-GQDs in other samples are similar with S-3 (see Fig. S1 for details, Figs. S1–S11 mentioned in this article can be obtained from the Supporting Information of the article). The most frequently observed size of S-3 was possibly given rise to further decomposition in the course of the second hydrothermal treatment (see preparation procedure in experimental section). High-resolution TEM images (Fig. 2e–h) indicate high crystallinity of both GQDs and S-3, revealing discernible lattice structures with a lattice space of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to an in-plane lattice spacing of graphene [25–28]. Therefore, GQDs and S-3 possibly share a similar pristine sp 2 carbon networked structure, which is similar to those of other reported GQDs [29–31].

Raman analysis is conducted to determine the form of the GQDs, S-3 in Fig. 3 a. Obviously, the D band around ∼1360 cm−1, G band around ∼1590 cm−1 and 2D peak at ∼2700 cm−1 of both GQDs and S-3, indicated the typical peaks of graphene-based materials. These peaks respects edge defects, highly ordered graphite, and layer of graphene. The intensity ratio of D and G peak (ID/IG) indicate the disorder density of graphene-based materials. Shown in Raman spectra, compared with that of GQDs (0.97), the ID/IG of S-3 is increased to 1.00, because of the unrepaired defects by inserting sulfur-containing functional groups. Moreover, the intensity ratio of 2D and G peak (I2D/IG) indicate the thick of graphene. The I2D/IG of S-3 is 0.68, where as that of GQDs is 0.64, indicating the layer of S-3 is thicker than that of GQDs, which is corresponding with the conclusion of AFM measurement.

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2) are used to determine the function groups of GQDs and S-GQDs. The absorption bands of the GQDs and S-GQDs at 3300–3500 cm−1 represent stretching vibrations of OH. The bands at 1590 and 1363 cm−1 represent the vibrational absorption band of CC and CN, respectively. The unique bands at 1126 cm−1 on the S-GQDs describe the sulfur-functionalized group. Especially, sulfur doping happens at the surface and edge, causing sulfur atoms to replace carbon atoms. These lead to the weaken CC signal of S-GQDs compared to that of GQDs. In addition, the surface groups of GQDs and S-GQDs were further investigated via XPS measurements. XPS full spectra (Fig. 3b) demonstrate that three peaks at 284 (C 1s), 400 (N 1s), and 533 eV (O 1s) are present in both GQDs and S-3 (O 1s XPS signal of S-GQDs is relatively high due to a contribution of sulfur-containing groups). Additional peaks at 168 (S 2p) and 229 eV (S 2s) observed in S-3 demonstrate successful doping S was into the framework of GQDs [32,33]. Table 1 shows the atomic analyses based on XPS measurements. Nitration of pyrene resulted in inevitable emergence of O and N in GQDs and S-3. The enhancement of O1s in all of S-GQDs samples may be ascribed to dope OH groups on the surface of S-GQDs in the hydrothermal process. On the contrary, S content reached as high as 19.53%, proving the efficiency of S doping by using powdered S. Further considering the atomic ratio of S to C (S/C) in a series of prepared S-GQDs, S/C ratio evidently varied in different samples. Fig. S3 shows the trend of the calculated S/C ratios in prepared S-GQDs. Markedly higher S coverage on C sp 2 skeleton of S-3 was observed compared with other samples for the reason that the former features lower C content (24.19 at.%) than the other samples. Based on these results, we conclude that S doping introduced more functional groups on the C sp 2 skeleton of GQDs, and that S-3 sample may be more suitable for synthesis of S-GQDs, as indicated by significantly transformed the structure of GQDs and their enriched physical properties.

High-resolution spectrum of the C 1s region of GQDs indicates the presence of three types’ carbon structure (Fig. S4): sp 2 C (CC) in graphene at 284.4 eV, sp 3 C in CN and CO at 285.6 and 286.2 eV, respectively, and CO at 288.1 eV from carbonyls and carboxylates [34]. The CC component showed the most intense peak among all deconvoluted peaks, indicating that CC serves as the main C bonding configuration constructing the graphitic matrix structure. As shown in Fig. 3b, the same carbon-containing groups existed, with an additional peak at 285 eV (CS) in sp 3 C of S-3. As appeared by the N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S5), pyrrolic N evidently represents the main N-binding configuration for S-3; this consequence agrees well with a previous report about pyrrolic N-doped graphene prepared by a hydrothermal approach [35]. Interestingly, S-3 presented the identical N-binding configuration as that of GQDs (Fig. S6). This phenomenon proves that S doping exerted a minor influence on other configurations. Nevertheless, high resolution of S 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 3c and d) of S-3 clearly shows two distinct S forms: thiophene S (CSC 2p 2/3, 163.4 eV; CSC 2p 1/2, 164.5 eV) and oxidized S (SO, 168.0 eV; SO n
, n = 2, 3, 169.0 eV) [36–38]. These results indicate that S was doped into graphene structure and instead of elemental crystals, formed thiophene units, which were physically adsorbed or wrapped within S-3; doped sulfur existed in two configurations, namely, thiophene sulfur and oxide-sulfur (shown in Fig. 1). On the basis of XPS results, the S-configuration content of S-GQDs sample with different amounts of doped S was quantitatively shown in Table 2 . Meanwhile, Fig. 4 summarizes portions of each S-configuration of prepared S-GQDs samples. As shown in the Fig. 4, S-doping occurred at basal planes and edges. During the reactive process, the doping happens at the surface and edge: (i) sulfur atoms replace some carbon atoms in graphene structure, forming CSC bands, (ii) sulfur atoms attach to the edge of graphene structure, forming SO bands. With changing S content, (i) the two configurations of S continuously switched; (ii) intensity ratio of oxide-sulfur/thiophene sulfur (labeled as I k2/I k1) of S-3 sample increased significantly (Fig. S7) in comparison with other samples. Therefore, the S-3 sample was the most successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal procedure.

Optical property characterization is typically used to study physical properties of carbon-based materials. Previously, researches rarely investigated light absorption and PL features of S-GQDs [39,40]. To select appropriate excitation wavelength for characterization of PL properties of GQDs and S-GQDs, we used UV–vis absorption as the function of solution concentration, as shown in Fig. 5 a and b. GQDs showed enhanced broad absorption band in the visible range (400–500 nm), as reported similarly in previous research [41,42]. By contrast, S-3 displayed an evident absorption decreasing in the visible region and a slight blueshift. In addition to broad absorption shoulder, strongest absorption at S-3 edge is located in the UV region (337 nm); this absorption corresponds to the n → π* transition of CO bond. Notably, the difference in UV–vis spectra between GQDs and S-3 indicate that coupling of S atom with GQDs results in the appearance of the strong absorption band in the UV region. Moreover, a monoexponential decay feature was observed from the PL decay curve of S-3 (Fig. 5c). The observed lifetime of the S-3 was 3.17 ns.

According to UV–vis absorption spectra, we selected the excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 490 nm for PL measurement. In general, excitation-wavelength dependence of emission wavelength and intensity comprises fundamental characteristics of carbon-based fluorescent materials [28]. In addition to affections of particles of different size, these behaviors are also influenced by the distribution of different emissive sites on each nanoparticle [43]. In our experiment, both GQDs and all of S-GQDs exhibited excitation-independent PL behaviors (see Fig. 6 a and Fig. S8). Different excitation wavelength induced different emission intensities, though the position of emission peak slightly transformed (<10 nm). Emission peaks of GQDs and S-3 were observed at ∼545 and ∼520 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6b, under 390 nm excitation, emission peak manifested a maximum shift of ∼20 nm. Accordingly, fluorescent color altered from bright green (GQDs) to bright blue (S-3, see insets in Fig. 5a and b). However, aside from these behaviors, several significant differences in optical properties were observed between S-3 and traditional GQDs [44]. The nearly excitation-independent emission wavelength of S-3 implies that both size and surface state of sp 2 clusters contained in S-3 should be uniform. As observed, S-3 showed a well-defined absorption band and PL band at 337 and 521 nm, respectively, with a narrow full width at half maximum of 30 and 52 nm, further proving the uniform size of sp2 clusters in S-3. Fig. S9 shows PL excitation (PLE) spectrum of S-3 after fixing the emission wavelength at 520 nm. Evidently, one of the excitation peaks is located at ∼386 nm; this observation conforms PL results. For absorption band and the emission peak of S-3 at 337 and 520 nm, respectively, Fig. 6b shows the PL spectra of GQDs and S-GQDs with different amounts of doped S after tuning the ratios of powdered S. After doping of S, the PL intensity of S-GQDs increases obviously. Interestingly, significant variation resulted from adding different mass ratios of S powders.

Fig. 7 a reveals that the distributions of the two S types for the S-GQDs obtained at the different weight of S powders. Based on the calculations, dependence of enhanced efficiency on the S powders weights and thiophene sulfur contents are shown in Fig. 7b and c, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7b, enhanced efficiency of S-1 is 16%, while a much higher enhancement of 73% is observed for S-3. With the increase of the amount of S powder, the disorder-induced defect states between gap of π and π* evidently decreased; by contrast, cluster-like states induced by the newly formed isolated sp2 domains appeared more frequently. The changed behavior lead the trend of the amount of thiophene sulfur. At the same time, the results of the fine scanned XPS also provided this trend. Interestingly, it is apparent that the enhanced efficiency of S-GQDs samples is almost proportional to the thiophene sulfur content (shown in Fig. 7c). On the basis of the results, it is reasonable to conclude that (i) thiophene sulfur can contribute to the fluorescence enhancement and (ii) the enhanced efficiency can be increased greatly with the increase of thiophene sulfur content. In other words, by tuning the thiophene sulfur content, it is possible to regulate the enhanced efficiency of S-GQDs samples.

To further confirm variation in optical properties by doping with S, we calculated PL QY using the results of absorption and PL intensity under 360 nm excitation. Table 3 shows QY results of S-GQDs with quinine sulfate as a reference. Values were calculated using the following equation [12]:
(1)
φ =
φ r
I /
I r
n 2
/
n r
2
A r
/ A
where φ represents QY, I corresponds to measured integrated emission intensity, n stands for refractive index (1.33 for water), and A indicates optical density. Subscript “r” refers to a reference of known QY. Absolute PL QY reached 57%, which is evidently higher than those in previous reports [23]. Based on these results, the S-3 sample exhibited the robustness PL emission intensity at the same concentration compared with other samples. Although fluorescence mechanism remains unclear, we deduce that quantity of defects contributes to emission intensity. When S atomic percentage of S-3 sample reaches 19.53%, the distance between adjacent defects becomes suitable for crossing and recombining (energized by photons) electrons and holes.

Fig. 8 a shows the energy level diagram of S-GQDs sample. The diagram represents additional energy levels between π and π* of graphene by S doping. Absorption of photons with energies of 3.7 (337 nm) and 2.9 eV (435 nm) induces two pathways of electron transition in S-GQDs, similar with the energies (3.8 eV) of work function (Fig. S10). With vibration relaxation and interband crossing, radiative recombination occurred, emitting photons with energies of 2.7 eV (460 nm) and 2.4 eV (520 nm), respectively. Based on Stokes shift, the emitted photons possessed lower energy compared with the absorbed ones [23]. Introduction of S provides additional pathways for electron transition in both absorption and PL emission. Evidently, the presence of S-related energy level significantly participates in tuning electronic structure and optical properties of S-GQDs.

We summarize the mechanisms that illustrate the evolution of PL (Fig. 8b) of S-GQDs with increasing S content. S-1 sample carried plentiful disorder-induced defect states between gaps of π and π* and exhibited a broad eminent PL emission peak centered at a longer-wavelength region under 386 nm, as shown in Fig. 8b (i). For clarification, we used S-5 as an example (other samples shown in Fig. S11). Accompanied by increasing S content, disorder-induced defect states between gaps of π and π* evidently decreased; on the contrary, cluster-like states induced by the newly formed isolated sp 2 domains appeared more frequently, as shown in Fig. 8b (ii). Electron-hole recombination among sp 2 cluster-like states induced increased intensity of fluorescence at shorter wavelengths. Notably, size of pristine sp2 domains determines emission wavelengths [45]; tunable fluorescence, which represents the main emission peak of S-GQDs, showed an evident blueshift from 515 nm to 505 nm and was possibly influenced by smaller sp 2 domains.

We develop two facile hydrothermal progresses to synthesize the low-cost, high-yield S-GQDs. Through regulating the content of S powders in S-GQDs synthesizing process, the optimal S-GQDs have a high S/C atomic ratio of 19.53%. The S doping introduce more functional groups on the C sp 2 skeleton of S-3 sample and result in the appearance of the strong absorption band in the UV region. The S-GQDs also exhibit overwhelmingly high fluorescence quantum yield (57%), which is evidently higher than those in previous reports about S-GQDs [20,46,47] and excitation-independent emission, resulting from the effect of the doped sulfur atoms. Moreover, the PL intensity of GQDs can be increased by doping it with S and the increasing efficiency depends on the thiophene sulfur content. This study provided an important new approach for developing future S-GQD for catalysis, optoelectronic devices and photosensing.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11764011, 21671129, 21571124, 51472241), the Shanghai Sailing Program (No. 16YF1404400), and Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2016GXNSFAA380008), P.R. China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.06.016.
